post_note: The post is Korean-only; there is no English post, so no /en/ link exists.
packaging_note: Executed 2026-07-15, packaged 2026-07-16 in the same firsthand session. The original demo repo (tmp/firsthand-jj/) and the downloaded jj.exe were removed per the finite-disk policy, but the authored harness (harness/setup.sh) and the recorded run output (harness/bisect-output.txt) were preserved, so this is the real harness, not a reconstruction. No fabricated artifact hashes.

environment.git: colocated repo (jj git init --colocate); plain git reads the same commits
environment.note: MAX_PATH gotcha on Windows: jj index segment filenames are ~128 chars, so the demo repo must live at a SHORT path (tmp/firsthand-jj) or jj hits `os error 3`. The bisect logic itself is OS-independent — the load-bearing variable is the jj version, not the OS.

subject.name: Jujutsu (jj) — Git-compatible VCS with automated bisect
subject.created_by: Martin von Zweigbergk (Google), Rust, uses the git on-disk format
subject.note: Pin 0.43.x in the post. `jj bisect run` itself was introduced in 0.33.0 (2025-09), NOT in 0.43 — the post says this plainly and does not sell bisect as brand-new. The genuinely recent surface is `jj file search` (0.41, 2026-05) and the 0.42/0.43 line. jj is still 0.x (pre-1.0).

method.harness: harness/setup.sh builds a linear 8-commit history in a colocated jj/git repo, planting a single off-by-one bug in mean() (divide by len-1) at the commit `refactor: tweak mean() internals`. The other commits (total/median/variance/docstring/minmax) are unrelated changes. It then runs `jj bisect run --range 'base..@'` with a one-line test and exercises `jj file search`, git colocation, and the operation log.
method.test: python -B -c "import stats,sys; sys.exit(0 if stats.mean([2.0,4.0,6.0])==4.0 else 1)"  (good=4.0, bug=6.0)
method.annotations: none — the demo is a plain Python fixture; jj drives the search, nothing is annotated.
method.recorded_output: harness/bisect-output.txt is the verbatim `jj bisect run` transcript from the 2026-07-15 run.

determinism.note: Which commit bisect reports as first-bad is deterministic: on a re-run it always narrows the 6 candidates in 3 evaluations to the planted `refactor: tweak mean() internals` commit, because the test is a pure function of the tree. What does NOT reproduce byte-for-byte is the jj change-id prefixes (xuspwrqt, yvvtsrol, ...) and git hashes shown in bisect-output.txt — jj assigns change-ids randomly per repo, so those strings are run-specific labels, not stable identifiers. Reproduction fidelity is about the METHOD and the first-bad description, not the id strings.

raw_artifacts.retention_policy: demo repo + jj.exe removed after the run (finite local disk)
raw_artifacts.note: tmp/firsthand-jj/ (the demo repo and jj index) and the downloaded jj.exe were deleted after the run. harness/setup.sh regenerates the whole demo on demand from a pinned jj 0.43.x. No artifact hashes were captured at run time, so none are invented — the committed harness + the recorded transcript are the reproduction.
